summary: Gruber reviews the new 16-inch MacBook Pro, focusing primarily on its keyboard and speakers as the most significant improvements. The keyboard returns to scissor switches with 1mm key travel, a hardware Escape key, and the inverted-T arrow layout, effectively correcting three years of butterfly keyboard mistakes. The speakers represent a generational leap he compares to the retina display transition. While the CPU is unchanged from the previous 15-inch model, the thermal system, GPU, and configurable RAM and storage all see upgrades. Gruber frames the release as Apple finally listening to professional users after a period of prioritizing aesthetics over function, calling it a MacBook you can once again argue is the best laptop money can buy.
key_points: The new scissor-switch keyboard with 1mm travel, hardware Escape key, and inverted-T arrows corrects every major complaint about the butterfly keyboards while retaining their best attributes like key stability and backlighting
The speakers represent a dramatic leap in quality comparable to the retina display transition, with force-canceling woofers that eliminate vibration and distortion even at high volume

Apple's keyboard changes implicitly concede that prioritizing aesthetics over function — a pattern Gruber links to the Jony Ive era — was a mistake, even as Apple carefully avoids saying so explicitly
The arrow key layout change perfectly illustrates Apple's form-over-function problem: full-size left/right arrows looked better but destroyed the tactile landmarks that made blind navigation possible
Despite using the same Intel CPUs as its predecessor, the laptop gains meaningful performance improvements through better thermals, new AMD GPUs, and up to 64GB RAM and 8TB SSD options
money_quotes: Calling it the 'Magic Keyboard' threads the impossible marketing needle they needed to thread: it concedes everything while confessing nothing.
Apple has always had a great keyboard that could fit in a MacBook — it just hasn't been in a MacBook the last three years.

I'm not sure I know anyone, though, who would disagree that over the last 5-6 years, Apple's balance of how things work versus how things look has veered problematically toward making things look better — hardware and software — at the expense of how they function.

No one would ever suggest that the steering wheel for a car be designed by people who don't drive. But yet somehow the entire Macintosh world has spent the last three years dealing with or avoiding keyboards that were seemingly designed by people who don't type.
This is the audio equivalent of going from chunky pixels to retina displays. It's that big a difference.
Really, I don't think there's anything I can write here that will convince you how good these speakers sound. However good you think I'm saying they sound, they sound way better than that.
The whole saga of the butterfly keyboards — their unreliable switches, poor typing feel, and anti-functional layout — betrays a certain arrogance.

We shouldn't be celebrating the return of longstanding features we never should have lost in the first place. But Apple's willingness to revisit these decisions — their explicit acknowledgment that, yes, keyboards are meant to be typed upon, not gazed upon — is, if not cause for a party, at the very least cause for a jubilant toast.
key_insight: The 16-inch MacBook Pro represents Apple correcting a multi-year mistake of prioritizing aesthetics over function, and while the fixes shouldn't need celebrating, Apple's willingness to reverse course is itself significant.
